content: As the Earth orbits the sun, its tilt causes different parts of the planet to receive varying amounts of sunlight throughout the year. Imagine a big ball, which is the Earth, tilted on its side as it moves around the sun. When the part of the Earth where you live is tilted towards the sun, you receive more sunlight and experience longer days, resulting in warmer temperatures and longer summers. Conversely, when your part of the Earth is tilted away from the sun, you receive less sunlight, and the days are shorter and colder, leading to winter. This tilt, about 23.5 degrees, is the key to understanding why we have seasons. As the Earth continues its orbit, different parts of the planet are tilted towards or away from the sun, creating the changing seasons we experience throughout the year.
